summary: The Docker-based reproduction starts the vulnerable application, forges a JWT token using the hardcoded default secret to bypass authentication, then sends a crafted MCP server configuration request to /api/mcp/cli/add with an attacker-controlled command field that is passed directly to spawn() without validation.
expected_security_impact: The crafted MCP add request should pass the attacker-controlled command field through to spawn('claude', cliArgs) without any validation, demonstrating that arbitrary commands can be injected into the MCP server configuration pipeline.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction verified the vulnerable code pattern in server/routes/mcp.js where cliArgs.push(command) passes user input directly to spawn(), confirmed the absence of any validation/sanitization, and demonstrated the attack chain with a forged JWT token.
verification_method: The verify.sh script (1) confirms the vulnerable spawn pattern in mcp.js, (2) confirms the hardcoded JWT secret enables auth bypass, (3) starts the server, (4) forges a JWT token, and (5) sends a malicious MCP add request verifying the server processes the injected command field.
evidence_produced_by_run: server/routes/mcp.js:91 - cliArgs.push(command) passes user input directly without validation
server/routes/mcp.js:109 - spawn('claude', cliArgs) executes with user-controlled arguments
No import of commandParser.js validateCommand in mcp.js - the existing validation utility is unused
Forged JWT token accepted by authenticateToken middleware using hardcoded secret
limitations_or_blockers: The spawn() call targets the 'claude' CLI binary which may not be present in the container; the vulnerability is in the user input reaching spawn() without validation, not in the spawn execution result

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
